title: My Initial Thoughts after finishing the series
display_title: My Initial Thoughts after finishing the series
wikitext:
[]: My Initial Thoughts after finishing the series             Quote Post by Stallard » Wed Jan 18, 2012 10:29 am Spoilers ahead, obviously. The first time I watched Evangelion was when I was very young, maybe five or six years old. As I remember it, I watched the first two episodes offhandedly during some cartoon network special and thought it was intriguing and refreshing. However, I never caught the name of the series and didn't know when it would air so I never watched any further. Fast forward about a decade and I suddenly stumble upon Evangelion again arbitrarily. I'm pretty sure I was just looking for a mecha anime after watching Gundam 00 and had a "Eureka!" moment when I suddenly realized I saw this before. I started watching again, but somehow didn't finish. So now it's about a year or two later and I've finally finished all 26 episodes and End of Evangelion. It's taken about a dozen years for me to finish watching this anime. But what do I think of it? I don't know, honestly. The first two episodes were badass. I loved the first battle and enjoyed the way in which it was told. But afterwards, the following episodes drastically tapered off in terms of its sense of urgency and danger (Only Asians would send their kids back to school in the apocalypse). The Angel design also started getting very bizarre. The first angel was a scary, humanoid beast. The second angel was a floating pink squid. And the third angel was a floating polygon. That's most of the reason why I stopped watching two years ago. But then they introduced Asuka and I was much more interested. She brought life to the series and made it fun to watch. I loved Asuka's character. Those vibrant, ostentatious girls with some sort of traumatic past is appealing to me for some reason. Her competitive nature with Shinji and clear contrast to Rei made for a great foil. Her flaws were realistic, her past believable. So I was able to continue through the next seven or eight episodes because of her. But the ending was a train wreck. One part of that was that Asuka became clinically depressed (which is sort of cute in a way) and nobody seemed to care. But without Asuka, there was no vibrance in the anime, no joy in watching it. Episodes 25 and 26 in the original Anime were just...bad. It made no sense and confused the viewer. I understand there were budget problems, but really? You're going to turn the last forty minutes of a series into some bizarre psychoanalysis that comes out of nowhere? With no indication of what happened before that? Even the End of Evangelion didn't satisfy me. Shinji goes back to being a whiny little bitch, which I thought he had overcome by now. And the fact that Eva-O1 rises up in an awesome light, sprouting wings of light to only be immediately captured was boring as hell. Then the Instrumentality project started again and I skipped forward because I really didn't want to watch any more psychoanalysis. So in the end, I thought the series was mediocre. I thought the story was pretty good, but I absolutely hated the characters within it (aside for Asuka <3). Shinji's unrealistic mentally ill and lonesome persona was a bore to watch in the psychoanalytic scenes. It may have been a point of the anime, but the fact that almost everybody separates themselves from each other, having no interaction with the other characters makes for a very dreary, depressing, cynical view of the world. The Human Instrumentality project was not very well explained either. What sort of motive compels people to unify people under one existence? I commend the concept of the Evas being biological weapons and I liked the setting of the anime. But somehow, despite the amount of playful banter and interaction between characters in the middle of the series, NGE ends with everybody being separate again, despite this instrumentality project. TLDR; I really watched because of Asuka. BONUS: Three WTF moments 1. Gay shower scene 2. "It's RAPING MY MIND" 3. Shinji, she's in a fucking coma.

[]: Re: My Initial Thoughts after finishing the series             Quote Post by Hyper Shinchan » Wed Jan 18, 2012 11:12 am Stallard wrote: Spoilers ahead, obviously.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Jan 18, 2012 12:33 pm I'm probably Asuka's most vocal fan around these parts, but I can't agree with your assessment of the series. The endings are actually pretty fantastic if you take them as they are and not as what you expect a finale to be. Yes, Shinji goes back to being a "whiny bitch", but that's to be expected given the end of the series. Part of the point the show was trying to make, and the core of what makes it realistic, is that you don't just "get over" the sorts of things the characters are experiencing. In real life those things can't be fixed. People who deal with them have to struggle with them for their entire lives, and Shinji does this. This is what makes him a heroic character: he keeps getting back up every time he's knocked down, and that makes for a worthy ending even with all of the psychoanalysis. The early part of the show is just the bait. It's there to suck you in and get you to think about what you're watching and what it means. But don't dismiss those scenes that put you off so easily; if you stick with them and really think about them you'll (likely) find they're a lot more rewarding than you might have initially expected.

[]:             Quote Post by Kamon-san » Wed Jan 18, 2012 12:52 pm I must agree with you, Bagheera. Like Stallard I was introduced to EVA at a much younger age and was ''sucked in'' by the action, interesting setting and characters, only to be shocked by the unusual (to say the least) last two episodes. I remember being so confused about what I had just witnessed, I felt I needed to rewatch the series again, going back to the first episodes and then watching it deconstructed in the most random of order. I would often find myself lost in thought, thinking back about various scenes of the series and trying to make sense of them. Luckily, a teacher I was good friends with back then was also a big fan of EVA and his analysis of the ending helped me shed some light on it. Of course at this point I understood that everyone had a different take on the meaning of this open ending. As for the movie ending... I only recently began to truly appreciate it. The first half of the movie with the MP Eva battle and NERV being taken over was fascinating, however just like the original finale the second half of the movie confused me some more. I knew after watching it only once that this movie would require a lot of time on my part to fully grasp. To be perfectly honest, when I began watching and reading EVA back then I had no idea what kind of ending the story would have. I couldn't imagine what would happen once the last Angel was defeated and I couldn't help worrying about which character would survive this apocalyptic setting. I guess my view of the story was very naive, expecting some sort of ''happy ending'' where Shinji, Ayanami and Asuka would survive all these hardships. Needless to say EVA is not that kind of series. ;P But even so, I found myself fascniated by the story from beginning to end. Perhaps you should indeed give the movie a second watch. If some of it confuses you, there is some very interesting analysis on this message board. =)

[]:             Quote Post by Hyper Shinchan » Wed Jan 18, 2012 1:27 pm Kamon-san wrote: Luckily, a teacher I was good friends with back then was also a big fan of EVA and his analysis of the ending helped me shed some light on it.

[]: Re: My Initial Thoughts after finishing the series             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Jan 18, 2012 1:34 pm Stallard wrote: But the ending was a train wreck.

[]: Re: My Initial Thoughts after finishing the series             Quote Post by Stallard » Wed Jan 18, 2012 1:36 pm Hyper Shinchan wrote: They were the deconstruction of Evangelion itself, some people say; personally I like them, they're quite useful to better understand the involved characters, the conclusion is weird and unexpected but I like it as well. Wrong choice, the Instrumentality is the core of that movie, it's actually quite interesting, even if I spent years trying to come to terms with it (without reaching any appreciable result). The world is quite often dreary, depressing and cynical, Evangelion obviously exaggerated it compared to the life of most people, because it's an anime, but it showed several issues that effectively afflict human life. It's the solution to those issues mentioned before, a plan conceived to complement every imperfect ego in one single, godlike, being. Also, haven't you skipped the part about Instrumentality in EoE? Personally I'd suggest you to watch EoE properly, also you could give a try to the new movies, unless you already did it.

[]: Re: My Initial Thoughts after finishing the series             Quote Post by Fireball » Wed Jan 18, 2012 3:13 pm Stallard wrote: And I suppose I am the same way. I expected some sort of light at the end of the tunnel, some sort of hope, which is part of the reason I disliked the ending.

[]: Re: My Initial Thoughts after finishing the series             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Jan 18, 2012 3:49 pm Stallard wrote: But they do.

[]:             Quote Post by BulletShoot » Wed Jan 18, 2012 4:21 pm Well like you I also first saw Neon Genesis Evangelion at a fairly young age (3rd or 4th grade I think) and like you I originally thought, robots, fighting, yes. Unlike it you though I quickly gained an appreciation for the bizarre and trippy designs for the angels (to be honest though the first time I saw Ramiel I was thrown for something of a loop). However despite my love for the awesome robots and surreal monsters, I for one actually got pretty interested in the plot and emotional content of the series. Sadly it would be another three or four years before I would actually finish the series (which in some ways is probably for the best) and I will admit that the first time I saw the ending I was a bit... confused. I was actually able to do what I believe to be a pretty good job at keeping up with it, until "Congratulations" and then I no longer had any idea what was going on. Then I watched End and while that did leave me with fewer unanswered questions, I still felt a little let down. Then some years latter when Rebuild came out I decided to go back and watch the series for old times sake and now I End of Evangelion is one of my favorite things. So basically what I feel I've ended up say here is that maybe you should try and let Eva digest (Zeruel) and then come back and see how you feel about it, and if you still don't care for it... oh well, no skin off my back.

[]: Re: My Initial Thoughts after finishing the series             Quote Post by Stallard » Wed Jan 18, 2012 4:53 pm Bagheera wrote: No, they don't. The best they manage is good coping mechanisms, and these aren't well-developed in 14-year-olds. When they have to kill people close to them those coping mechanisms go out the window.

[]: Re: My Initial Thoughts after finishing the series             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Jan 18, 2012 5:24 pm Stallard wrote: Oh, I had forgotten about Kaworu. That explains Shinji's state at the end a little bit better but I still find it absurd that Shinji's character chooses to stay depressed in ludicrous situations. When NERV headquarters was being blown up and stormed by thousands of troops, Shinji's still curled up into a ball and Misato has to physically drag him around.

[]: Re: My Initial Thoughts after finishing the series             Quote Post by esselfortium » Wed Jan 18, 2012 5:36 pm Stallard wrote: The Instrumentality Project seemed fundamentally flawed, at least to somebody of my philosophy, and I didn't quite understand that either. The combination of all existence into one superexistence didn't make sense to me. Of what purpose would this serve? The majority of the population is not so insecure that such a drastic action is warranted.

[]: Re: My Initial Thoughts after finishing the series             Quote Post by chee » Wed Jan 18, 2012 5:47 pm Stallard wrote: The Instrumentality Project seemed fundamentally flawed, at least to somebody of my philosophy, and I didn't quite understand that either. The combination of all existence into one superexistence didn't make sense to me.

[]:             Quote Post by Kamon-san » Wed Jan 18, 2012 6:05 pm This discussion actually makes me wonder if a topic where people share what they thought the ending to EVA could have been does exist? Now that I look back on what I imagined the ending would be when I first began the series, I'm curious to find out what others imagined too... Yes, but like I said they can all come back. 3I was the global reset button, for better or worse.

[]: Re: My Initial Thoughts after finishing the series             Quote Post by TehDonutKing » Wed Jan 18, 2012 6:20 pm Stallard wrote: Then the Instrumentality project started again and I skipped forward because I really didn't want to watch any more psychoanalysis.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Jan 18, 2012 6:24 pm Kamon-san wrote: I am aware this was discussed several times on the message board so I won't make my point drag for too long, however I believe this is relevant to Stallard's disappointment and confusion for the ending. When I first watched the End of EVA final scene and saw Asuka and Shinji alone on the beach I truly got the impression they would be the last surviving humans. I do remember Yui explaining that if people desire everyone can ''come back'' but somehow the atmosphere of the ending just gave me an awful impression. Of course, after reading several different opinions here my impression of that ending changed, but I can still understand why so much people felt like they were let down by this conclusion.

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Wed Jan 18, 2012 6:29 pm Episodes 25 and 26 are some of the best parts of the series, btw. Also, I've been considering watching the new movies, but I'm hesitant if they are just another retelling of the main story.

[]: Re: My Initial Thoughts after finishing the series             Quote Post by symbv » Wed Jan 18, 2012 8:53 pm Stallard wrote: I suppose part of my dissatisfaction with the ending is disparities with my own philosophy.

[]:             Quote Post by Stallard » Wed Jan 18, 2012 9:00 pm Thanks for the replies - honestly, I think my disappointment at the ending is more or less a kneejerk reaction for some naive hope for some happier ending. The motives behind the Instrumentality Project makes more sense when explained properly (or explained at all in my case). and Evangelion does deserve praise for having a very thin line between good and evil. It is realistic in the sense that the people with money and power are the ones who win out in the end.
